title: Correlation
description: Relationship Status Correlation quantifies the strength and direction of the relationship between two variables. It is the bedrock of predictive modeling. If рЭСЛ X is correlated with рЭСМ Y, then knowing рЭСЛ X helps us predict рЭСМ Y. But beware: correlation can lie. It can show relationships that don't exist, hide relationships that do, and most dangerously, suggest causation where there is none. Covariance: The Raw Ingredient Before Correlation, we must understand Covariance. It measures how two variables change together. рЭРґ рЭСЬ рЭС£ ( рЭСЛ , рЭСМ ) = 1 рЭСЫ вИТ 1 вИС рЭСЦ = 1 рЭСЫ ( рЭСЛ рЭСЦ вИТ рЭСЛ ЋЙ ) ( рЭСМ рЭСЦ вИТ рЭСМ ЋЙ ) Cov(X,Y)= nвИТ1 1 вАЛ вИС i=1 n вАЛ (X i вАЛ вИТ X ЋЙ )(Y i вАЛ вИТ Y ЋЙ ) Cov > 0 X up, Y up. They move together. Cov < 0 X up, Y down. They move opposite. Cov вЙИ 0 No linear relationship (could still be non-linear!). Problem with Covariance: It's not scaled. If you measure height in meters, Cov is X. If you measure in centimeters, Cov is 10,000X. The number itself is meaningless without context. Correlation Coefficient ( рЭСЯ r) Correlation is standardized covariance. We divide by the product of standard deviations to get a unitless number between -1 and 1. рЭСЯ = рЭРґ рЭСЬ рЭС£ ( рЭСЛ , рЭСМ ) рЭЬО рЭСЛ рЭЬО рЭСМ = вИС ( рЭСЛ рЭСЦ вИТ рЭСЛ ЋЙ ) ( рЭСМ рЭСЦ вИТ рЭСМ ЋЙ ) вИС ( рЭСЛ рЭСЦ вИТ рЭСЛ ЋЙ ) 2 вИС ( рЭСМ рЭСЦ вИТ рЭСМ ЋЙ ) 2 r= ѕГ X вАЛ ѕГ Y вАЛ Cov(X,Y) вАЛ = вИС(X i вАЛ вИТ X ЋЙ ) 2 вАЛ вИС(Y i вАЛ вИТ Y ЋЙ ) 2 вАЛ вИС(X i вАЛ вИТ X ЋЙ )(Y i вАЛ вИТ Y ЋЙ ) вАЛ Interpreting r r = 1.0 Perfect positive. Points on a line. r = 0.7 Strong positive. Clear trend. r = 0.3 Weak positive. Noisy but visible. r = 0.0 No linear relationship. r = -0.8 Strong negative. Inverse trend. Pearson vs Spearman Not all relationships are straight lines. Choosing the right correlation measure depends on your data. Pearson Correlation ( рЭСЯ r) Measures linear relationships. Requires both variables to be approximately normal. Sensitive to outliers (one bad point can destroy your r). If рЭСМ = рЭСЛ 2 Y=X 2 , Pearson may be near 0 even though relationship is perfect. Spearman Rank Correlation ( рЭЬМ ѕБ) Measures monotonic relationships (always increasing or decreasing). Converts values to Ranks (1st, 2nd, 3rd) then runs Pearson on ranks. Robust to outliers and non-normal data. Perfect for ordinal data (ratings 1-5). Case Study: Bulb Factory Quality Control The Question Is there a relationship between the voltage supplied during manufacturing and the lifespan of the light bulb? If so, we can optimize the voltage setting. The Data We sample 50 bulbs, record the manufacturing voltage (V) and the hours until failure (H). Pearson r 0.72 Spearman ѕБ 0.68 p-value < 0.001 The Interpretation Strong positive correlation. Bulbs manufactured at higher voltage tend to last longer. But wait! Does higher voltage cause longer life? Or do better quality filaments (which last longer) also happen to tolerate higher voltage? We need to run an experiment (A/B test) to prove causation. Interactive Simulator Adjust the correlation coefficient. See how the cloud of points tightens into a line as рЭСЯ вЖТ 1 rвЖТ1 or рЭСЯ вЖТ вИТ 1 rвЖТвИТ1. Notice how at рЭСЯ = 0 r=0, the cloud is a perfect circle. CORRELATION (R) 0.00 -1.0 (SAME) 0.0 (NONE) +1.0 (SAME) SHAPE No Correlation (Random) N = 400 | Gaussian Noise Note: At r=0, points form a circular cloud (ѕГ=1). As |r| вЖТ 1, the Y-noise diminishes, collapsing the cloud onto the regression line. Correlation вЙ† Causation This is perhaps the most important lesson in statistics. Just because A and B move together, doesn't mean A causes B. There are several possibilities: 1. A causes B (Direct Causation) Rain causes wet ground. Smoking causes cancer. 2. B causes A (Reverse Causation) Cities with more police officers have higher crime rates. Does hiring police cause crime? No, high crime rates cause cities to hire more police. The effect (crime) is driving the apparent cause (police presence), not the other way around. 3. C causes both A and B (Confounding Variable) Ice cream sales and drowning deaths are correlated. Summer (heat) causes both. This is the most common trap. 4. Pure Coincidence (Spurious) With enough variables and time series, you'll find spurious correlations by chance. Spurious Correlations Sometimes, things correlate purely by accident. These are hilarious to look at but dangerous if you take them seriously. "The number of people who drowned by falling into a pool correlates with films Nicolas Cage appeared in." (Real stat: r = 0.66 from 1999-2009). Bulb Example: A Warning You notice that bulbs manufactured on Fridays have higher defect rates. Before blaming the day of the week, check if Friday is when the junior shift works, or if Friday is when the machine gets its weekly maintenance (and isn't fully calibrated afterward). The correlation is real, but the cause is something else entirely. ML Applications Feature Selection We want features that correlate highly with the target (predictive power) but low with each other (no redundancy). A correlation matrix heatmap is your first step. Multicollinearity In Linear Regression, if two features are perfectly correlated (e.g., "Temperature in ¬∞C" and "Temperature in ¬∞F"), the matrix рЭСЛ рЭСЗ рЭСЛ X T X becomes singular and cannot be inverted. You must drop one. VIF (Variance Inflation Factor) VIF measures how much the variance of a regression coefficient is inflated due to multicollinearity. рЭСЙ рЭРЉ рЭРє > 10 VIF>10 is a red flag. Calculate as 1 / ( 1 вИТ рЭСЕ рЭСЦ 2 ) 1/(1вИТR i 2 вАЛ ) where рЭСЕ рЭСЦ 2 R i 2 вАЛ is R-squared of regressing feature i on all other features. PCA Preprocessing If features are highly correlated, PCA can transform them into uncorrelated principal components. This helps with numerical stability and can reduce overfitting. Previous: ANOVA Next: Resampling Methods
